jokes.text: Were you watching me sleep? / No, I was watching you try to breathe.
jokes.setup: Abbi wakes up and asks if Lesley was watching her sleep — a mildly creepy but romantic-coded question.
jokes.punchline: Lesley reframes it as watching her 'try to breathe,' undercutting romance with clinical observation.
jokes.explanation: Clean subversion of the romantic 'watching you sleep' trope. The pivot to medical observation is quick and economical. Not groundbreaking but a well-executed cold open beat.

jokes.text: I think you have a badly deviated septum. But it's a damn cute deviated septum.
jokes.setup: Lesley has been watching Abbi struggle to breathe while sleeping.
jokes.punchline: Diagnoses her with a medical condition and immediately softens it with an affectionate compliment, treating 'cute deviated septum' as a coherent thing.
jokes.explanation: The absurdist pivot of 'cute deviated septum' as a term of endearment is genuinely funny and odd. It establishes Lesley as someone who romanticizes in a clinical, unusual way. Good character intro joke.

jokes.text: Suggestion to use a bobby pin 'like they do with the... yarmulkes?' leading to: 'Why do you know that word? / I'm Jewish.'
jokes.setup: Lesley suggests a bobby pin to keep the hat in place like 'they' do — the pause implies she's searching for the word.
jokes.punchline: Abbi fills in 'yarmulkes' and then asks why Lesley knows the word, as if it's obscure — which flips when Lesley reveals she's Jewish, making the question absurd.
jokes.explanation: Two-beat structure works well: Abbi's question is funny because it implies 'yarmulke' is obscure knowledge, then Lesley's answer makes Abbi's question retroactively dumb. Good economy.

jokes.text: No. I'm... I'm 100% Jewish. / Well, you are funny.
jokes.setup: Lesley emphasizes she is 100% Jewish.
jokes.punchline: Abbi's response 'well, you are funny' invokes the Jewish comedian stereotype as if it's meaningful evidence — treating it as confirmation rather than a cliché.
jokes.explanation: Plays on the Jewish comedian stereotype but does so knowingly — Abbi is being affectionate and slightly dumb simultaneously. A little lean on the stereotype but Abbi's earnestness saves it.

jokes.text: Yeah, no shit. Ever seen a mirror?
jokes.setup: Ilana excitedly announces her Genes&Me results show she's 100% Ashkenazi Jew.
jokes.punchline: Her mother's deadpan 'yeah, no shit, ever seen a mirror' treats the DNA reveal as the most obvious thing imaginable.
jokes.explanation: Perfectly economical. The mother's bluntness is character-specific and the line reframes expensive DNA testing as completely pointless for someone with an obviously identifiable heritage. 'Ever seen a mirror' is sharp.

jokes.text: Rapid-fire genetic disease list: anxiety, poor eyesight, Tay-Sachs, cystic fibrosis, Fragile X syndrome — each met with 'check' or 'uh-huh.'
jokes.setup: Ilana reads off traits from her genetic report starting with relatable ones (anxiety, poor eyesight).
jokes.punchline: The list escalates from mild to genuinely devastating genetic diseases while Ilana continues with the same breezy energy and her mom adds 'uh-huh' as if confirming grocery items.
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'anxiety' to 'Fragile X syndrome' while maintaining the same casual tone is the joke. The structure is clean and the mom's 'uh-huh' as emotional flatline is perfect character work. Dark without being mean-spirited.

jokes.text: I could've told you all of that without your selling your medical history to the government. / Google owns your DNA, you naive little bitch!
jokes.setup: Ilana's mom is dismissive of the DNA testing service.
jokes.punchline: 'Google owns your DNA, you naive little bitch' — the conspiracy-adjacent paranoia delivered with maternal affection, ending on 'little bitch' as a term of endearment.
jokes.explanation: This line is exceptional. The paranoid tech-skepticism is both culturally resonant and perfectly in character for Ilana's mom. 'Naive little bitch' as maternal affection is a signature Broad City move — raunchy language used warmly. Genuinely quotable and the character specificity is high. Justifies the 8.0 craft score.

jokes.text: 'Endophenotypes: The Genetic Inheritance of Mental Disorders' as a grad school essay title, met with 'Yikes!'
jokes.setup: Ilana announces she'll write her grad school personal essay about inherited trauma.
jokes.punchline: The overly clinical, jargon-heavy title — combined with mom's single-word 'Yikes!' — captures the absurdity of academizing one's own dysfunction.
jokes.explanation: 'Yikes' as the only response to a genuinely alarming academic title is well-timed and characterizes the mom well. The joke works because it's understated — mom doesn't argue, just reacts.

jokes.text: I'm related... ♪ to a Holocaust survivor ♪ — sung operatically.
jokes.setup: Ilana reads further into her DNA results and makes a discovery.
jokes.punchline: She announces the connection to a Holocaust survivor by breaking into operatic song — treating genealogical news like a musical reveal.
jokes.explanation: The tonal whiplash of singing about Holocaust connection is quintessential Ilana — she's not being disrespectful, she's being overwhelmed with joy in the most inappropriate register possible. The absurdist musical choice is very character-specific.

jokes.text: Maybe I should get my DNA tested. I could be related to a Holocaust survivor, too. / Who, a guard?
jokes.setup: Abbi (not Jewish) suggests she might also be related to a Holocaust survivor.
jokes.punchline: Ilana's immediate 'who, a guard?' — implying Abbi's European heritage might connect to perpetrators rather than victims.
jokes.explanation: One of the sharpest jokes in the episode. Perfect economy — three words that completely reframe the scene. It's dark, it's historically charged, but delivered with the breezy Ilana confidence that makes it land as friendship-roast rather than malicious. The setup is natural and the punchline is totally unexpected. Genuinely excellent. Justifies 8+ score.

jokes.text: Come on, Ilana. I don't mean a full-on Nazi. I mean like a Polish soldier who looked the other way.
jokes.setup: Abbi defends herself against 'who, a guard?' accusation.
jokes.punchline: Her defense is that she didn't mean a 'full-on Nazi' — just someone complicit by inaction. This self-correction makes things significantly worse, not better.
jokes.explanation: A perfect escalation of joke 11. Abbi's attempted walk-back reveals she hasn't thought this through at all — 'a Polish soldier who looked the other way' is somehow worse than what she was denying. The inadvertent self-incrimination is a classic comedy structure executed cleanly.

jokes.text: I am Jewish. / Yeah, emphasis on the 'ish.'
jokes.setup: Abbi insists she is Jewish (implying she has some connection to justify her claim).
jokes.punchline: Ilana's 'emphasis on the ish' — a clean wordplay joke that dismisses Abbi's Jewishness as marginal.
jokes.explanation: The pun isn't wholly original but the delivery context makes it land hard. It's economical and perfectly timed as a capper to the Nazi exchange. High quotability — feels like a line people would repeat.

jokes.text: I watch your eyes when we walk, when we talk. You've never even looked at a hat.
jokes.setup: Ilana questions whether Abbi is genuinely a hat person.
jokes.punchline: The revelation that Ilana monitors Abbi's eye movements to track where her interests lie — presented as normal friend behavior.
jokes.explanation: This is deeply character-specific to Ilana — her obsessive devotion to Abbi manifesting as tracking her gaze like a hawk. Treating this surveillance as romantic rather than creepy is central to their dynamic. The specificity ('when we walk, when we talk') elevates it.

jokes.text: Do you think you're gonna be a hat person for the rest of your life? / I don't know. / Are we talking about hats? / Yeah. We're talking about hats.
jokes.setup: Ilana's increasingly pointed questions about the hat become obviously about Abbi's relationship with Lesley.
jokes.punchline: Both confirm they're talking about hats — the meta-denial of the subtext is the joke. The audience knows they're not talking about hats.
jokes.explanation: The extended hat-as-sexuality metaphor is a well-executed running bit. The closing 'yeah, we're talking about hats' functions as a winking acknowledgment of the subtext. Not subtle, but Broad City isn't going for subtlety here — it's going for warmth.

jokes.text: We are here to visit my very dear, sweet, and close 16th cousin Saul Borowitz.
jokes.setup: Ilana and Abbi arrive at what appears to be an assisted living facility.
jokes.punchline: The over-the-top familial warmth ('very dear, sweet, and close') applied to a 16th cousin she just found out about via DNA app.
jokes.explanation: Functional character joke — Ilana immediately overclaims emotional connection. Not exceptional but consistent with her character and sets up the Saul storyline.

jokes.text: Looks like I'm the magician even though you're the one wearing that hat.
jokes.setup: Ilana does something (apparently knocks on the door in a special way or gestures dramatically) that opens an unlocked door.
jokes.punchline: Self-congratulatory 'magician' comment deflected onto Abbi's absurd hat — continuing the hat visual gag.
jokes.explanation: Adequate callback to the hat gag. The 'magician' setup is a bit thin and the punchline relies entirely on the hat's visual absurdity which the transcript alone can't fully convey.

jokes.text: Ilana's 'magic trick' to open the door — Abbi: 'I think the door was just unlocked'
jokes.setup: Ilana announces she'll try something before they knock
jokes.punchline: Whatever flourish she performed was just... opening a door that was already unlocked. Abbi's deflation is the joke.
jokes.explanation: A classic Broad City visual gag that relies on performance. Ilana treating her accidental success as wizardry, then Abbi immediately contextualizing it — good two-hander beat. The transcript suggests the physical bit without spelling it out.

jokes.text: Saul playing dead — Ilana screams 'No! No, no, no, no. No. Oh, God. I knew I shouldn't have stopped at Sephora before I came here!'
jokes.setup: Ilana and Abbi enter Saul's room. He appears motionless.
jokes.punchline: Ilana's panic is immediately (and absurdly) redirected to self-blame about stopping at Sephora — her priorities are hilariously misaligned with the apparent death scenario.
jokes.explanation: The Sephora guilt is a perfectly Ilana detail — she would genuinely feel guilty about Sephora in a moment of crisis. It's the right non-sequitur for this character and lands as a bigger laugh than the fake death itself.

jokes.text: Gotcha! Works every time.
jokes.setup: Ilana and Abbi believe they've walked in on a dead man.
jokes.punchline: Saul immediately reveals he was pranking them — and 'works every time' suggests this is a practiced routine, implying he does this frequently enough to have confirmed data.
jokes.explanation: 'Works every time' is the key addition — it characterizes Saul immediately as a wily, mischievous survivor who pranks all his visitors. Good character introduction in three words.

jokes.text: You know, you must be one of those Genes&Me kids. I can see the resemblance. / That's me as a horny teenager. See the hair?
jokes.setup: Saul recognizes Ilana as a DNA-test relative and produces a photo to show the resemblance.
jokes.punchline: The photo is labeled 'me as a horny teenager' — Saul introducing himself via teenage horniness as a defining characteristic.
jokes.explanation: Establishes Saul as an unfiltered, sexually frank old man — a classic comedic archetype but done with specific Broad City irreverence. 'Horny teenager' coming from a Holocaust survivor is a tonal choice that humanizes him immediately.

jokes.text: She's my everything. But, you know what, I'll just boil it down to soulmate.
jokes.setup: Ilana introduces Abbi to Saul and trails off trying to define their relationship.
jokes.punchline: 'I'll just boil it down to soulmate' — implying 'soulmate' is the simplified version of something even more vast.
jokes.explanation: Very Ilana. The joke is that 'soulmate' is being treated as the reductive, insufficient description of something larger. Deeply in character and touching while still being funny.

jokes.text: I experience anxiety diarrhea three to eight times daily.
jokes.setup: Ilana is asking Saul about somatic expressions of inherited trauma in her grad school interview framing.
jokes.punchline: The clinical delivery of 'three to eight times daily' — the specificity of the range is what makes it funny, as if she's tracked this.
jokes.explanation: The quantification is the joke. Ilana doesn't say 'I have anxiety diarrhea' — she says three to eight times daily, establishing a data range. This is high-craft raunchy humor: the specificity creates the comedy.

jokes.text: Saul's response 'Ay, yi, yi, yi' to the anxiety diarrhea revelation.
jokes.setup: Ilana describes her anxiety diarrhea as a somatic expression of inherited trauma.
jokes.punchline: Saul's exasperated old-Jewish-man reaction — not horrified, not amused, just exhausted.
jokes.explanation: A reaction beat that works by being understated. The audience expects a bigger reaction; the casual 'ay yi yi' suggests Saul has heard worse. Characterizes him well.

jokes.text: I'm gonna write 'possible connection.'
jokes.setup: Ilana asks if Saul's Holocaust trauma 'tracks back' to her anxiety diarrhea. Saul says no.
jokes.punchline: Undeterred by the flat denial, Ilana notes she'll write 'possible connection' — the academic hedge in service of a predetermined conclusion.
jokes.explanation: Excellent joke. The comic logic is airtight: Ilana is writing an academic essay and will not let contradictory evidence derail her thesis. 'Possible connection' as the epistemically dishonest hedge is both funny and a sharp satire of motivated academic reasoning. Very quotable and deeply in character.

jokes.text: Nazi shit, anything.
jokes.setup: Ilana asks Saul to tell her about mental illness history, stories from the old country.
jokes.punchline: The list ends with 'Nazi shit, anything' — the casual, almost bored addition of 'Nazi shit' as a content category.
jokes.explanation: The casualness of 'Nazi shit, anything' in the same breath as 'stories from the old country' is characteristically Ilana — she treats horrifying historical events with the same casual tone as everything else. It's irreverent but not disrespectful.

jokes.text: I'm sorry, Saul. We can't. I mean, we're just following orders. / Well, I've heard that one before.
jokes.setup: Abbi apologetically tells Saul they can't take him off the floor because they're 'following orders.'
jokes.punchline: Saul's immediate 'well, I've heard that one before' — invoking the Nuremberg defense used by Nazi war criminals, making Abbi's innocent phrase suddenly loaded.
jokes.explanation: This is the standout joke of the episode. The setup is perfect — Abbi says 'following orders' completely innocently with no awareness of how it lands. Saul's response is delivered with a weary, darkly comic precision that transforms the moment. It's structurally impeccable: the audience gets the connection a half-second before he says it, which makes the delivery even more satisfying. Dark comedy at its best — using historical horror as character-defining material rather than shock value. Saul's whole existence as a survivor is compressed into four words.

jokes.text: Saul's response: 'Well, I've heard that one before.' — then beat, then Ilana immediately capitulates and says they're breaking him out
jokes.setup: Saul reacts to 'just following orders'
jokes.punchline: The devastatingly understated 'Well, I've heard that one before' — and the collective realization in the room that Ilana just said the worst thing
jokes.explanation: The single best line reading in the episode and arguably the episode's peak comedic moment. 'Well, I've heard that one before' is six words that do the work of an entire history lecture. It's devastating, it's funny, it's character-perfect for a Holocaust survivor who has presumably had to live with the banality of people using that phrase. The economy score of 9.5 is justified — this is close to the 'Snip, snap' level of maximum impact per word, just in a different register. The gap between Saul's calm and the enormity of what he's referencing is the engine of this joke.

jokes.text: Okay, we're getting you out of here right now! — Ilana's immediate overcorrection after the 'following orders' moment.
jokes.setup: Saul drops the 'following orders' line, which clearly hits Ilana hard.
jokes.punchline: Her immediate, guilt-driven pivot to 'we're getting you out RIGHT NOW' — she responds to a darkly comic comment as if she personally needs to atone.
jokes.explanation: Good payoff to joke 27. Ilana's guilt-driven overcorrection is funny because it makes her complicit in breaking the rules she was just upholding. Also characterizes her as someone who gets emotionally swept up instantly.

jokes.text: Oh, uh... this guy had an accident. Shit was everywhere. We gotta get rid of the rug.
jokes.setup: Ilana and Abbi are attempting to sneak Saul out of the facility in what appears to be an elaborate, ridiculous scheme involving a rug.
jokes.punchline: Ilana's cover story — 'this guy had an accident, shit was everywhere' — is simultaneously a plausible nursing home explanation and a comically undignified cover for Saul.
jokes.explanation: The cover story is funny in context — using a fecal accident as a plausible nursing home excuse to smuggle out an elderly man in a rug is absurd and very Broad City. Loses some points for relying on poop humor as a crutch.

jokes.text: Smell that New York air. It's like garbage. I love it!
jokes.setup: Saul emerges outdoors after being smuggled out of the facility.
jokes.punchline: He rhapsodizes about New York air and specifically appreciates that it smells like garbage.
jokes.explanation: The 'garbage smell as freedom smell' inversion is charming. Saul's unfiltered appreciation for sensory experiences — even bad ones — is consistent with his character as someone who's been cooped up.

jokes.text: Saul at the shoe store, sees high tops, says 'I'll be right back' and wanders off mid-interview.
jokes.setup: Ilana sits Saul down in pleasure (a shoe store) and immediately launches into grad school interview question #1 about PTSD.
jokes.punchline: Saul ignores the question entirely because he sees high tops — establishing that he will consistently derail Ilana's academic agenda with his own desires.
jokes.explanation: Sets up the running dynamic: Saul is not here to be Ilana's trauma resource, he's here to live his best day. The high tops are the first instance of this pattern and establish the comedic premise of the rest of the episode.

jokes.text: Ilana's interview question mid-shoe shopping: 'Okay, Saul, now that I've got you in pleasure, interview question number one — what does post-traumatic stress disorder mean to you?'
jokes.setup: They've taken Saul shoe shopping as a treat
jokes.punchline: Ilana pivots from 'pleasure' to PTSD research mid-Foot Locker — the juxtaposition is excellent, and 'now that I've got you in pleasure' is an unusually clinical way to say 'now that you're enjoying yourself'
jokes.explanation: 'I've got you in pleasure' is a funny phrase — it sounds slightly predatory but means 'I've made you comfortable.' The pivot to PTSD while surrounded by sneakers is good tonal whiplash.

jokes.text: Girls! I found it... a dope-ass pair.
jokes.setup: Ilana and Abbi are waiting; Saul has wandered off.
jokes.punchline: The dramatic 'Girls! I found it...' builds to something profound — but it's shoes. His pride in 'dope-ass pair' is completely genuine and delivered with the same gravity as a major discovery.
jokes.explanation: The misdirection setup (dramatic announcement) paid off with sneakers is well-constructed. 'Dope-ass pair' in Saul's voice is funny because it's such contemporary slang from a Holocaust survivor. Good character joke.

jokes.text: What about Red Hook? / Red Hook is kind of hard to get to, right? / Well, we can always take the... / Shut up, Saul!
jokes.setup: They're deciding where to eat; Red Hook is raised as an option.
jokes.punchline: Abbi notes it's hard to get to. As Saul (who has apparently lived in NYC) begins to offer a transit solution, Ilana immediately shuts him down — she doesn't want to go to Red Hook.
jokes.explanation: The 'Shut up, Saul!' is a good beat — the reversal of expected deference to elders. Ilana bossing around a Holocaust survivor about Red Hook transit is a specific NYC comedy beat.

jokes.text: Alan motherfucking Cumming.
jokes.setup: They spot a celebrity on the street.
jokes.punchline: The full-name expletive introduction — 'Alan motherfucking Cumming' — delivered with religious reverence.
jokes.explanation: The specific choice of Alan Cumming as an object of reverence is very Broad City — niche enough to feel authentic rather than playing to the widest possible audience. The expletive middle name construction is a reliable comedic format but executed with genuine enthusiasm.

jokes.text: Alan Cumming created his own perfume line called 'Cumming.' Is that LOL genie or what? / I'm wearing it right now.
jokes.setup: Ilana is listing Alan Cumming's credentials to Saul.
jokes.punchline: The perfume named 'Cumming' is introduced, then immediately revealed that Ilana is wearing it — of course she is.
jokes.explanation: The perfume joke is elevated by 'I'm wearing it right now' — the reveal that Ilana has committed to this purchase is exactly the kind of detail that makes Ilana Ilana. Also 'LOL genie' is a characteristically Ilana non-sequitur phrase.

jokes.text: Pre-9/11, pre-'Sex in the City,' Basquiat, Keith Haring New York.
jokes.setup: Ilana is making the case to Saul for why they must follow Alan Cumming.
jokes.punchline: The slight misquote 'Sex in the City' (instead of 'Sex and the City') is either a character error or a deliberate jab — either way, it's funny. The larger joke is Ilana invoking Basquiat and Keith Haring to justify following a celebrity.
jokes.explanation: 'Sex in the City' is a recurring Ilana mistake across the series and always lands as character comedy. The speech itself is slightly bloated but characterizes Ilana's cultural pretension well.

jokes.text: Ilana's description of what Alan Cumming represents: 'The pre-9/11, pre-Sex in the City, Basquiat, Keith Haring New York.'
jokes.setup: Trying to explain Alan Cumming's cultural significance to Saul
jokes.punchline: The list is slightly wrong ('Sex IN the City,' not 'and the City'), and the cultural references being rattled off with authority to a Holocaust survivor who has survived literal history is a tonal mismatch joke
jokes.explanation: The 'Sex IN the City' error is a recognizable real-world mistake that might be intentional. Mild tonal comedy in explaining downtown cool-person history to a WWII survivor. Not a strong standalone joke.

jokes.text: The only Haring I'm interested in is pickled herring. And as soon as we can get to that, I am fine.
jokes.setup: Ilana invokes Keith Haring as a symbol of authentic New York. Saul hears 'Haring.'
jokes.punchline: Saul's wordplay on Haring/herring — pivoting from avant-garde street art to traditional Jewish food — is a generational joke that also reveals his priorities.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the best jokes in the episode. The Haring/herring pun is perfect — it works phonetically, it works culturally (Jewish food vs. avant-garde art), and it perfectly encapsulates who Saul is versus who Ilana thinks he should be. 'As soon as we can get to that, I am fine' is Saul at his most dignified. The character specificity and economy are both exceptional.

jokes.text: 'Giddy-up, Saul! Come on! We got it, Saul. Shit, shit! Here we go. We're losing him. Okay. Alright.'
jokes.setup: They're trying to catch Alan Cumming before he disappears
jokes.punchline: The physical comedy of two young women trying to keep pace with Alan Cumming through NYC streets while also managing a 90-year-old in new LeBrons — 'Giddy-up, Saul' treating him like a horse
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy that works better visually. 'Giddy-up, Saul' is the verbal gag — calling your 90-year-old Holocaust survivor relative a horse. Functional scene comedy.

jokes.text: 'Drag brunch! Drag brunch! We must! We must!'
jokes.setup: They're following Alan Cumming and he's entered a drag brunch establishment
jokes.punchline: Ilana's 'We must!' is simultaneously true (they cannot not go to drag brunch if Alan Cumming is there) and absurd — and bringing a Holocaust survivor to drag brunch
jokes.explanation: The premise of bringing an elderly Holocaust survivor to drag brunch is the joke — 'We must!' is the right energy. Not a crafted joke so much as a situation joke.

jokes.text: Drag brunch song lyrics: '♪ Jiggly Caliente is the best / Tuck your dick between your legs / And shake those unfeasibly large breasts ♪'
jokes.setup: Entering a drag brunch with a nonagenarian Holocaust survivor
jokes.punchline: The lyrics are the punchline — specifically 'unfeasibly large' as the adjective choice, which is very specific and British-inflected
jokes.explanation: 'Unfeasibly large' is the standout word choice — the comedy is in that specific adjective applied to drag breasts. The song works as a set piece joke more than a crafted joke.

jokes.text: Can I get a coffee with a side of you? — Saul hitting on the drag performer.
jokes.setup: The drag performer asks if they can take their order.
jokes.punchline: Saul immediately flirts — establishing him as romantically and sexually alive at 91, and completely comfortable in drag brunch.
jokes.explanation: The flirtation line itself is a fairly standard move, but the character doing it (91-year-old Holocaust survivor at a drag brunch he was just smuggled out of a nursing home to attend) gives it outsized impact.

jokes.text: Oh, Saul, this is a marijuana pen. Ooh, sorry. You probably don't want that. / Why shouldn't I get high?
jokes.setup: Ilana offers her vape pen to the group, then immediately second-guesses offering it to Saul.
jokes.punchline: Saul's indignant 'why shouldn't I get high?' — the assumption that an elderly Holocaust survivor wouldn't want weed is treated as an insult.
jokes.explanation: The subverted ageism is the joke: Ilana (of all people) applies a conservative assumption to Saul, and he refuses it. 'Why shouldn't I get high?' has a righteous dignity to it that's both funny and character-affirming.

jokes.text: Get ripped, 'cuz.
jokes.setup: Saul asks why he shouldn't get high.
jokes.punchline: 'Get ripped, 'cuz' — Ilana blessing a Holocaust survivor to get stoned using contemporary slang and a family term of endearment.
jokes.explanation: Three words that work perfectly. The slang ('ripped'), the casual permission-granting, and the affectionate 'cuz' all click together. Very quotable line that captures the spirit of the Ilana-Saul relationship.

jokes.text: You can't get off unless you cough! That's for damn sure.
jokes.setup: Saul takes a hit from the weed pen.
jokes.punchline: Saul knows the rule about coughing and weed — delivered with folksy authority, as if this is a life proverb. A Holocaust survivor sharing weed wisdom at drag brunch.
jokes.explanation: Works because Saul delivers it with the authority of someone imparting hard-won wisdom. The incongruity of the source and content is the joke, and it's well-executed.

jokes.text: Conga time! Ooh, come on, girls.
jokes.setup: Saul has just taken a hit of weed at drag brunch.
jokes.punchline: He immediately initiates a conga line — the weed kicks in instantaneously (or he was already this way), and his instinct is a conga.
jokes.explanation: The conga line is a visual/physical comedy moment that works better on screen than on paper. Saul leading a conga at drag brunch while stoned is a strong image. Loses points for being described rather than seen.

jokes.text: It's a bare... with a hint of lavender.
jokes.setup: Ilana approaches Alan Cumming at the bar.
jokes.punchline: She opens with a fragrance note — presumably about the 'Cumming' perfume she's wearing, delivered as a come-on or an introduction. The context makes it both sexually charged and absurd.
jokes.explanation: A callback to the perfume joke that lands because Ilana leads with the perfume note as an opening to Alan Cumming himself. The phrase 'bare with a hint of lavender' describing a perfume called 'Cumming' carries obvious double meaning, delivered with perfect Ilana obliviousness/intentionality.

jokes.text: Thank you so much for bringing us here today. / For bringing you? / We followed you for, like, 13 blocks. / Cool.
jokes.setup: Ilana thanks Alan Cumming for 'bringing them' to the drag brunch.
jokes.punchline: He correctly points out they stalked him. His response 'cool' to being told they followed him 13 blocks is the perfect understated celebrity-encountering-fans reaction.
jokes.explanation: Alan Cumming's 'cool' is perfectly calibrated — not alarmed, not flattered, just accommodating. The meta-acknowledgment of celebrity stalking as a normal fan activity is both honest and funny.

jokes.text: We really need to cherish our elders and hear their stories. / You want to meet him?
jokes.setup: Abbi makes an earnest statement about cherishing elders.
jokes.punchline: Alan Cumming takes it at face value and offers to meet Saul — immediately cutting against the theoretical nobility of Abbi's statement by testing it.
jokes.explanation: A transitional joke — functional but not exceptional. Works as a beat to move to the next gag (Saul disappearing).

jokes.text: Saul has vanished. Abbi and Ilana turn around and he's gone — 'He's gone for fucking ever!'
jokes.setup: Ilana is about to introduce Saul to Alan Cumming. They turn to show him off.
jokes.punchline: Saul has completely disappeared mid-scene — the first instance of the running gag of losing the Holocaust survivor.
jokes.explanation: The disappearing act is a well-timed visual beat. 'Gone for fucking ever' is a great line — the dramatic certainty of it when they just had him is funny. Sets up the lost-Saul running gag.

jokes.text: We cannot be losing Holocaust survivors! Not now that the world is gaining Nazis somehow!
jokes.setup: Ilana and Abbi panic about losing Saul.
jokes.punchline: The political observation embedded in the panic — 'not now that the world is gaining Nazis somehow' — contextualizes the absurd day trip within actual contemporary horror.
jokes.explanation: One of the best lines of the episode. It's funny, it's true, and it does something rare: it connects the personal (losing Saul) to the political (the rise of white nationalism) in a way that doesn't feel preachy because it's wrapped in panic. The 'somehow' is perfect — disbelieving, ironic, and angry all at once. Easily quotable and thematically resonant.

jokes.text: Ilana's method of locating a lost Holocaust survivor: 'Where would a Holocaust survivor go? Um... pickled herring. Um... Zabar's!'
jokes.setup: They need to figure out where Saul went
jokes.punchline: Ilana's deductive process — Holocaust survivor → pickled herring → Zabar's — is both reductive and actually correct logic
jokes.explanation: Good callback to Saul's pickled herring comment. The deductive chain is funny in its reductiveness and in being correct — Ilana's stereotype-based logic actually works. Zabar's as the endpoint is very New York-specific which adds to the joke.

jokes.text: Where would a Holocaust survivor go? Um... Zabar's!
jokes.setup: Ilana and Abbi need to find Saul and think through where he'd go.
jokes.punchline: The 'think like Saul' exercise concludes with Zabar's — a famous Upper West Side Jewish deli — as if this is obvious profiling.
jokes.explanation: The logic is funny — it's ethnic profiling framed as empathy. Broad City is allowed to make this joke in this context. The beat lands because Ilana says it with complete confidence, no hedging.

jokes.text: Shit! My hat, dude! / Leave the hat! / No! It's not that simple!
jokes.setup: They're about to rush to Zabar's to find Saul.
jokes.punchline: Abbi's immediate concern for the hat (which has been an object of ambivalence all episode) is both a callback and a character moment — 'it's not that simple' treating hat abandonment as philosophically complex.
jokes.explanation: 'It's not that simple' about a hat she's been ambivalent about all episode is the joke — the hat has become meaningful not because she loves it but because abandoning it means something about her relationship. Good callback.

jokes.text: Abbi and Ilana distracted by babka and whitefish salad while searching for Saul.
jokes.setup: They're urgently searching for a lost Holocaust survivor.
jokes.punchline: They get sidetracked by the food at Zabar's — ordering a pound of whitefish salad 'to go, we're kind of in a hurry.'
jokes.explanation: The contradiction of 'urgent mission' and 'I'll take a pound of whitefish salad to go' is good situational comedy. Consistent with how these characters operate.

jokes.text: The resilience of the Jewish people is just astounding. / Yeah. / We got to keep educating ourselves, you know. / Truly never forget. / Shit! Saul!
jokes.setup: Emerging from Zabar's, they pause to reflect on Jewish resilience and education.
jokes.punchline: The solemn declaration 'truly never forget' immediately followed by 'Shit! Saul!' — they just forgot Saul again while discussing never forgetting.
jokes.explanation: This is the best structural joke in the episode. 'Never forget' is the foundational Holocaust remembrance phrase, and they say it with genuine reverence — then immediately demonstrate they've literally forgotten the Holocaust survivor they're responsible for. The juxtaposition is perfect, the timing is impeccable, and it's both funny and thematically pointed. The structure earns a 9. One of the standout moments of the episode.

jokes.text: Okay, where else might an old Jew go? / A sale. / Genius. Jew magnet! Yes!
jokes.setup: They've lost Saul again and are thinking through where he'd go.
jokes.punchline: Abbi suggests 'a sale'; Ilana's 'Jew magnet!' response validates the ethnic stereotype with complete self-inclusion (as a Jew herself).
jokes.explanation: Intra-community joke that works because both characters are Jewish (or Jewish-adjacent) and the stereotype is played as affectionate self-awareness. 'Jew magnet' is a specific enough phrase to land. The joke is a bit lazy on its own but functions fine in sequence.

jokes.text: Shopping montage — Abbi and Ilana distracted by a sale while searching for Saul.
jokes.setup: They go to a sale to find Saul, confident in the 'Jew magnet' theory.
jokes.punchline: They get completely absorbed in the sale themselves — 'you don't find great clothes, great clothes find you' — while Saul is still missing.
jokes.explanation: The running gag of them losing Saul while getting distracted by the thing they were using to find him is consistent and funny. 'Great clothes find you' as philosophy is a nice character detail.

jokes.text: He's at IKEA. / What? How do you know that? / When he breaks out, he always goes to IKEA. That's what he does.
jokes.setup: Abbi and Ilana confess to the nursing home staff that they've lost Saul.
jokes.punchline: Doris immediately and calmly says 'he's at IKEA' — the revelation that this has happened before and IKEA is Saul's known escape destination is both funny and raises enormous questions.
jokes.explanation: Excellent surprise revelation. The specificity of IKEA is perfect — not a deli, not a park, IKEA. Doris's complete lack of alarm ('that's what he does') characterizes this as routine, which makes it funnier. Also sets up the explanation scene at IKEA. High craft score for structure and economy.

jokes.text: Didn't I tell you not to leave the third floor with him? / You did mention that. / I didn't really hear it. / I feel like that was more directed to my friend, to be honest.
jokes.setup: Doris holds them accountable for taking Saul off the floor.
jokes.punchline: Ilana deflects responsibility to Abbi; Abbi says she 'didn't really hear it' — both versions of not-my-fault delivered simultaneously.
jokes.explanation: The parallel deflection responses are a solid bit — each character reveals their excuse personality simultaneously. Not exceptional but clean.

jokes.text: And... And... And... And we could just take... / We'll just take our licenses back real quick, and then we'll just be like, 'Great meeting ya.' / No.
jokes.setup: Abbi and Ilana are trying to figure out how to exit the awkward situation with Doris.
jokes.punchline: Ilana floats the idea of just leaving ('great meeting ya') after losing a resident; Doris's single 'no' is devastating in its flatness.
jokes.explanation: Doris's 'No' after a long stammering attempt to escape is perfect deadpan. The escalating stammer from Ilana vs. the flat monosyllabic response is a well-structured cringe beat.

jokes.text: You know, y-you come in for one thing, and... and... / Like your cousin, the Holocaust survivor.
jokes.setup: Saul is at IKEA, apparently distracted by it the way everyone is distracted by IKEA.
jokes.punchline: Saul trails off mid-'IKEA loses you' observation; Abbi's 'like your cousin, the Holocaust survivor' is a mordant deadpan completion that acknowledges they came to IKEA for a cousin and got absorbed too.
jokes.explanation: Abbi's completion is surprisingly sharp — the casual appending of 'Holocaust survivor' to a mundane IKEA observation lands well. Good deadpan beat.

jokes.text: You can leave the hat in the cart.
jokes.setup: Saul is about to sit down for Swedish meatballs with the girls.
jokes.punchline: Unprompted, Saul tells Abbi she can leave the hat in the cart — he, a 91-year-old Holocaust survivor with decades of perspective, has clocked that the hat is bad.
jokes.explanation: The hat callback delivered by Saul is particularly satisfying because he's the most credible judge — someone who's lived through actual horror can tell you the hat is bad. Delivered casually as if it's obvious. High marks for character integration and economy.

jokes.text: What's up with the IKEA fetish, Saul? ... when I escaped from the camp, I wound up in Sweden, and those people were so good to me.
jokes.setup: They ask Saul why he always goes to IKEA. The setup has been comic — it's framed as a 'fetish.'
jokes.punchline: The genuine, moving answer — Saul escaped to Sweden and the Swedes were kind to him. IKEA is a connection to that kindness and survival. The comedy is replaced by genuine feeling, and that tonal shift IS the comedic payoff.
jokes.explanation: This is the emotional and comedic apex of the episode. The entire IKEA thread is built as a running joke — Saul's eccentric escape destination — and the payoff recontextualizes everything. The furniture was elegant and the Swedish people were good to him. It's genuinely moving AND retroactively makes all the IKEA comedy resonate at a deeper level. This is when the show earns its dark comedy credentials — not just by making Holocaust jokes but by making them matter. The craft score of 9.0 reflects exceptional structure (the long setup pays off beautifully), maximum character integration, and the complete absence of cheapness.

jokes.text: Saul's motto: 'Never forget. Also... don't remind me.'
jokes.setup: Saul references the Holocaust Memorial Museum they visited
jokes.punchline: 'Never forget. Also... don't remind me.' — the complement to 'Never forget' being 'don't remind me' — a survivor's authentic ambivalence about memory
jokes.explanation: The best joke of the episode, and one of the better constructed jokes in the show's run. 'Never forget' is the most loaded cultural shorthand in Jewish memory — a commandment, a vow. 'Also... don't remind me' is its perfect, heartbreaking, funny inverse. Six words that capture the entire ambivalence of trauma survival: you must remember, but the remembering costs you. The pause before 'don't remind me' is crucial — Saul isn't joking, or he is, and that uncertainty is the richest possible comedic space. This is what a 9+ looks like: a line that couldn't come from any other character, that means multiple things simultaneously, that is maximally economical, and that will stay with you. Completely earned through the episode's careful treatment of the Holocaust material.

jokes.text: Never forget. / Also... don't remind me.
jokes.setup: Ilana references the Holocaust Memorial Museum they visited. Saul responds with the foundational Holocaust remembrance phrase.
jokes.punchline: 'Also... don't remind me.' — the private, personal addendum to the public motto. For Saul, 'never forget' applies to others; he'd prefer not to be reminded himself.
jokes.explanation: The best joke in the episode and among the finest in the series. 'Never forget. Also, don't remind me.' is structurally perfect — it takes the most sacred phrase of Holocaust remembrance and adds a deeply human, survivor-specific addendum that is simultaneously funny, true, compassionate, and devastating. It could only be said by Saul. It cannot be said by anyone who hasn't lived it. The economy is flawless: four words that contain multitudes. This is what 9.4 craft looks like — it approaches the cultural-touchstone tier while remaining specific to this character and this show.

jokes.text: And you look a great deal like my father. / Are you serious? / Yeah. It's all about the eyes... the moustache.
jokes.setup: Saul studies Ilana's face and says she looks like his father.
jokes.punchline: Implying Ilana has a moustache — or features coded masculine. The 'eyes... the moustache' beat implies the visual of Ilana's signature look including upper lip hair.
jokes.explanation: The moustache implication is vintage Broad City body comedy — referencing Ilana's famously unselfconscious grooming choices. Saul delivering it without judgment as a sign of family resemblance is sweet and funny. The pause before 'the moustache' is good comic timing.

jokes.text: Abbi breaks up with Lesley's hat in the IKEA — 'I feel like I gave this guy, like, a good go of it today. And I just don't think it's really me.'
jokes.setup: Abbi has been ambivalent about the hat all episode. Now in an IKEA, she decides.
jokes.punchline: She uses relationship-breakup language to end things with a hat — 'gave it a good go,' 'it's just not my...' — treating hat-abandonment with the gravity of ending a romance (which is what she may actually be doing symbolically).
jokes.explanation: The hat break-up scene pays off the extended metaphor. Treating the hat's 'rejection' with the care of ending a relationship is consistent with the episode's subtext. Works better thematically than as a pure comedy beat.

jokes.text: Abbi... listen. I thought it looked cute on you. But you're much cuter without it.
jokes.setup: Abbi is trying to explain her hat feelings to Ilana.
jokes.punchline: Ilana reveals she always thought the hat looked bad — her support has been performative all along. The retroactive confession reframes all of Ilana's 'brave' and monitoring behavior as barely concealed disapproval.
jokes.explanation: Good payoff to the entire hat thread. 'But you're much cuter without it' is both a callback resolution and a genuine Ilana expression of love — she supported the hat because she supports Abbi, but now she can finally be honest.

jokes.text: Sweet relief. / Oh, my God. / I feel so much better.
jokes.setup: Abbi removes the hat.
jokes.punchline: Her physical relief at removing it — 'sweet relief' — as if she's been in pain rather than wearing a hat.
jokes.explanation: A satisfying capper to the hat gag — melodramatic relief sells the idea that the hat has been a genuine burden. Functional resolution comedy.

jokes.text: Ilana's grad school essay — 'It's not about the hideous hat. It's about the head that wears it.'
jokes.setup: Ilana reads from her essay over footage/voiceover of the day.
jokes.punchline: The essay opens with 'it's not about the hideous hat' — validating that the hat was indeed hideous while using it as a thesis metaphor.
jokes.explanation: The phrase 'hideous hat' as an academic essay opening is funny. It confirms what everyone suspected (the hat is terrible) while pivoting to the actual thesis. Good economy.

jokes.text: I used to think being Jewish meant IBS, Tay-Sachs, inherited trauma, and childhood contact lenses.
jokes.setup: Ilana's essay opens with her prior understanding of Jewish identity.
jokes.punchline: The list reduces Jewish identity to a medical profile ending with 'childhood contact lenses' — a mundane optometric detail that deflates the preceding serious conditions.
jokes.explanation: The list structure mirrors the earlier Genes&Me results reading, and 'childhood contact lenses' as the capper is perfect absurdist understatement. The list ends where it shouldn't — with the most mundane, everyday Jewish-kid experience.

jokes.text: ...carry a stag table to the parking lot of an IKEA at 91 years old.
jokes.setup: Ilana's essay is listing what Jewish resilience means, getting progressively more specific.
jokes.punchline: The essay culminates in Saul carrying flat-pack IKEA furniture to a parking lot at age 91 as an example of Jewish resilience.
jokes.explanation: This is excellent comedic writing — it takes the emotional IKEA reveal and adds one more absurdist layer (actually carrying the STAG table out). The specificity of 'stag table' is perfect. Holocaust survival and IKEA furniture as parallel acts of resilience is both funny and oddly moving.

jokes.text: And I hope you have the strength to accept this little Jew into your Psychology Graduate program. Shalom.
jokes.setup: Ilana's essay closes.
jokes.punchline: The pivot from invoking Holocaust resilience to applying to grad school ('accept this little Jew') is the joke — the grandiosity of the setup for the mundane goal of grad school admission. 'Shalom' as the essay sign-off is perfect character detail.
jokes.explanation: The essay closing is both funny and surprisingly sincere. 'This little Jew' is self-deprecating and endearing; 'Shalom' as a grad school essay sign-off is an extremely Ilana touch. The structure of leveraging genuine tragedy for personal advancement is the satirical engine of the whole episode, and the essay crystallizes it.

jokes.text: Yeah, I'm getting in.
jokes.setup: Ilana finishes reading her essay aloud.
jokes.punchline: Her complete confidence — 'yeah, I'm getting in' — as if the essay is objectively perfect.
jokes.explanation: The self-assurance is pure Ilana. The audience both agrees and disagrees that the essay is good, which is the tension that makes it funny. Economy is excellent — four words.

jokes.text: Are you performers? / No. Why? / The... hat. / Oh, this? No, this is a style choice. / Usually, I'm pro-choice, but I would abort this if I could.
jokes.setup: A stranger asks if they're performers, nodding at Abbi's hat (which she has put back on for some reason).
jokes.punchline: Ilana's abortion joke — 'usually I'm pro-choice but I would abort this if I could' — applies reproductive rights language to hat opinion.
jokes.explanation: One of the funniest lines of the episode and the series. The construction is excellent: 'usually I'm pro-choice' establishes the political stance before flipping it as a hat verdict. The joke requires Ilana's specific feminist politics to work — it's completely character-specific. The audacity of applying abortion language to fashion opinion is peak Broad City. High quotability score — this is the kind of line that gets clipped and shared.

jokes.text: The hat... I'd burn it.
jokes.setup: Ilana says she'd abort the hat.
jokes.punchline: The stranger — who has no skin in this game — simply says 'the hat... I'd burn it' with complete detached certainty.
jokes.explanation: The stranger's endorsement of burning the hat is a good capper — a third-party validator who's never met these people delivers the final verdict with the authority of someone whose opinion was never solicited. The escalation from 'abort' to 'burn' is a good comedy sequence.
